title: ConvertKit vs Substack (2025): Which Is Better?
meta_description: Compare ConvertKit and Substack. See pricing, features, and which email platform suits creators best.
h1: ConvertKit vs Substack: Complete Comparison for Creators
intro: ConvertKit and Substack are both popular email platforms designed for creators, but they serve different needs and business models. ConvertKit focuses on comprehensive marketing automation and audience building, while Substack emphasizes direct monetization through paid subscriptions. Choosing between them depends on your primary goal: building a sustainable creator business or launching a newsletter with built-in payment infrastructure.

comparison_table.tool_b: Free or revenue-share (10% of paid subscriptions)
Built-in paid subscriptions, no affiliate program

choose_a_if: You need advanced email automation and subscriber segmentation for nurturing campaigns
You plan to sell digital products, courses, or merchandise alongside your newsletter
You want a traditional email marketing platform with comprehensive creator tools
choose_b_if: You're a writer focused on monetizing directly from your audience through subscriptions
You want unlimited free subscribers with no tier restrictions
You prefer a simpler, more distraction-free publishing platform

faq.a: Neither is universally better—it depends on your goals. ConvertKit excels for creators building multi-product businesses with advanced automation, while Substack is superior for writers monetizing through direct subscriptions. ConvertKit has higher G2 ratings (4.4 vs 4.0), suggesting stronger satisfaction among power users.

faq.q: Which is cheaper, ConvertKit or Substack?
faq.a: Substack is cheaper if you're monetizing subscriptions, as it operates on a 10% revenue-share model with no upfront costs. ConvertKit starts free but requires $29/month for paid plans. For non-monetized newsletters, Substack's unlimited free tier beats ConvertKit's 1,000-subscriber limit.

faq.q: Can I switch from Substack to ConvertKit?
faq.a: Yes, most email platforms allow subscriber exports. You can export your Substack subscriber list and import it into ConvertKit, though you may lose some metadata. Plan your migration carefully to maintain subscriber engagement during the transition.
